narrative:
As an Asian male applicant, your law school application was evaluated using a model trained on data from thousands of students who took the bar exam, focusing on factors like academic records, test scores, and family background to predict first-time pass rates. The model assessed your profile— including your undergraduate GPA of 2.70, LSAT score of 31, full-time student status, upper-middle family income, and notably low Year 1 and Year 3 school decile rankings of 1—and predicted a 91.7% probability that you would fail the bar exam on your first attempt. This high failure risk directly led to the rejection of your admission, as the school prioritizes applicants likely to succeed on the bar to maintain their strong pass rates.

The most influential factor pushing the prediction toward failure was your Year 3 decile ranking, which placed you in the bottom 10% of your school that year—far below the typical student's middle-of-the-pack average around the 5th or 6th decile. Next in importance was your Year 1 decile, also a bottom-10% ranking compared to the average of about the 6th decile, signaling early academic struggles that the model sees as a strong warning sign for bar success. Your upper-middle family income had a smaller but still notable push toward the failure prediction, as the model associates certain income levels with outcomes less favorable than those of lower or higher brackets on average.

These top three factors outweighed positives like your full-time enrollment status, which is common among successful students, and stood out starkly against your LSAT and GPA, both below typical admits. In essence, your consistently low class rankings in key undergraduate years dominated the model's view, painting a picture riskier than most applicants who land in higher deciles. While your background as an Asian male doesn't directly factor in, the model focuses purely on these performance metrics shared across all students.

To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to the 37 average, strengthening your academic record through additional coursework or a post-bac program to improve future decile rankings, and exploring schools with holistic reviews that might weigh your full-time commitment more heavily. Gathering letters of recommendation highlighting your resilience could also help reframe your story in a new application cycle. With targeted improvements, you can shift the odds in your favor for the next round.
